en.extract:
The southern black korhaan (Afrotis afra), also known as the black bustard, is a species of bird in the bustard family, Otididae. This small bustard is found in southwestern South Africa, from Namaqualand, south to Cape Town and east to Makhanda. It prefers semi-arid habitats such as grasslands, shrublands and savannas where it can easily prey on ground-dwelling arthropods and eat seeds. It reproduces yearly in the spring and will lay about one or two eggs per breeding season.
Numbers have declined rapidly as much of its habitat has been converted to agricultural land and remaining tracts are often fragmented. Due to this habitat destruction, the species is considered vulnerable by the IUCN Red List.


Taxonomy
The southern black korhaan was one of the many bird species originally described by Carl Linnaeus in the 1758 10th edition of his Systema Naturae, where it was given the binomial name of Otis afra. Formerly known as Eupodotis afra, it is now classified as Afrotis afra.  They belong to the bustard family, Otididae.
It was previously thought that the southern black korhaan and the northern black korhaan were the same species (the northern black korhaan being a sub-species, E. afra afroaoides). They are now classified as different species due to their disjunct ranges and habitats, and differences in plumage, vocalizations and social systems. They also have differences in their mitochondrial DNA.
"Southern black korhaan"  has been designated as the official common name for the species by the International Ornithologists' Union (IOC).


Description
The southern black korhaan is a ground-dwelling bird with some sexual dimorphism. The male's main body plumage is of a checkered black and white with a black underbelly and neck. It also displays some white on the underwings, on the cheeks and in stripes over the eyes. The female, in contrast, has the whole of her head, neck and breast the same colour as her body, which is checkered brown and white.  Both the female and the male have bright yellow legs.
The male is slightly larger than the female. The male has a wingspan of 27 cm (10.6 in) to 28 cm (11.0 in) and the female a wingspan of 25 cm (9.8 in) to 26 cm (10.2 in). The tail is about 12 cm (4.7 in) to 13 cm (5.1 in) long. The male's bill is larger with a length of 3.8 cm to 3.9 cm (1.5 in), while the female's is about 3.5 cm (1.4 in) long. The bill is of a reddish colour with a black tip. Lifespan averages 10 years.


Distribution and habitat
The southern black korhaan is endemic to southwestern South Africa, ranging from Namaqualand, south to Cape Town, and east to Makhanda. As of 2020, its estimated suitable habitat throughout its range is approximately 20,355 km2 (7,859 sq mi). It prefers open, semi-arid habitats such as shrub-lands and savannahs where it can easily prey on grass-dwelling arthropods, also grasslands adjoining marshland.


Behaviour


Vocalisation
Vocalisation has been described as a "continuous cackling sound". It is mostly the male that calls, as it vocalizes very often and very loudly. The call is a raucous "knock-me-down, knock-me-down".


Breeding
The southern black korhaan is a polygynous species, which means males mate with multiple females. To attract females, males will display high flying. They breed in the spring. Females lays one or two eggs in a depression in the soil and covers them with strands of grass. The eggs are of an olive or brown colour with some dark black spots. Only the females provide parental care as they take care of the eggs and then raise the chicks.


Diet

This is an omnivorous species. Two-thirds of its diet is made up of arthropods, and it will eat termites, beetles, grasshoppers and ants. The rest of its diet is composed of plant matter, mostly seeds. It partakes in a mutualistic relationship with Acacia cyclops, a species of Acacia; the southern black korhaan benefits from the seeds as they are a readily available food source, and in return, disperses the seeds to good germination sites. The southern black korhaan will also ingest grit and other small rocks to help in digestion by assisting the grinding process in the gizzard. A study has also shown that two thirds of birds have nematode parasites in their intestinal tract.


Status and conservation
Recent studies have shown that the southern black korhaan's population has been decreasing lately, which has raised concerns about its conservation status. The species was once very common but it is now becoming rarer as its habitat is being fragmented. Hence, it has been deemed vulnerable by the IUCN Red List of threatened species since October 1, 2016.
The primary threat to these species is the conversion of natural vegetation to agricultural lands as well as aquaculture. This has caused habitat fragmentation and a reduction in available food sources. This reduction in habitat also means less suitable breeding grounds, which has not only affected breeding success, but chick and egg survival rates. Indeed, agricultural lands do not provide sufficient plant cover to protect them from predators such as the pied crow.
Other threats include climate change, human disturbance and diseases.


References


External links

Southern Black Bustard (Afrotis afra) - Alive: Handbook of the birds of the world
Southern Black Bustard (Afrotis afra) - Birdlife International
Southern Black Bustard vocalization - e-Bird
Southern Black Bustard vocalization and distribution - Xeno-canto
Southern Black Korhaan photo gallery - VIREO (Drexel University)
Southern Black Korhaan (Outarde Korhaan) - Oiseaux.net

The '''southern black korhaan''' ('''''Afrotis afra'''''), also known as the '''black bustard''', is a species of bird in the bustard family, [[Otididae]].<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal|last1=del Hoyo|first1=Josep|last2=Collar|first2=Nigel J.|date=2014|title=Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International digital checklist of the birds of the world|journal=Lynx Edicions and BirdLife International|volume= 1: Non-passerines}}</ref> This small bustard is found in southwestern [[South Africa]], from [[Little Namaqualand|Namaqualand]], south to [[Cape Town]] and east to [[Makhanda, Eastern Cape|Makhanda]].<ref name="iucn status" /> It prefers semi-arid habitats such as grasslands, shrublands and savannas where it can easily prey on ground-dwelling arthropods and eat seeds.<ref name="iucn status" /><ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|last1=Kok|first1=O. B.|last2=Earlé|first2=R. A.|date=1990-12-01|title=Diet of the Black Korhaan Eupodotis Afra in the Orange Free State and North-West Cape|journal=Ostrich|volume=61|issue=3–4|pages=107–110|doi=10.1080/00306525.1990.9634155|issn=0030-6525}}</ref> It reproduces yearly in the spring and will lay about one or two eggs per breeding season.<ref name=":6">{{Cite book|title=Roberts - Birds of southern Africa, VIIIth edition|last1=Hockey|first1=P.A.R.|last2=Dean|first2=W.R.J.|last3=Ryan|first3=P.G.|publisher=The Trustees of the John Voelcker Bird Book Fund|year=2005|location=Cape Town}}</ref>

Numbers have declined rapidly as much of its habitat has been converted to agricultural land and remaining tracts are often fragmented.<ref name="iucn status" /><ref name=":7">{{Cite book|title=Impacts of environmental change on large terrestrial bird species in South Africa: insights from citizen science data|last=Hofmeyr, Sally D|date=2012|publisher=University of Cape Town|oclc=920646225}}</ref> Due to this [[habitat destruction]], the species is considered vulnerable by the [[IUCN Red List]].<ref name="iucn status" />

== Taxonomy ==
The southern black korhaan was one of the many [[Aves in the 10th edition of Systema Naturae|bird species originally described]] by [[Carl Linnaeus]] in the 1758 [[10th edition of Systema Naturae|10th edition]] of his ''[[Systema Naturae]]'', where it was given the [[Binomial nomenclature|binomial name]] of ''Otis afra''.<ref>{{cite book |last=Linnaeus |first=Carl |author-link=Carl Linnaeus |title=Systema Naturae per Regna Tria Naturae, Secundum Classes, Ordines, Genera, Species, cum Characteribus, Differentiis, Synonymis, Locis. Tomus I. Editio decima, reformata |volume=v.1 |publisher=Laurentius Salvius |location=Holmiae (Stockholm, Sweden) |language=la |year=1758 |page=155 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/727061}}</ref> Formerly known as ''Eupodotis afra'', it is now classified as ''Afrotis afra.''<ref name=":3" />  They belong to the bustard family, Otididae.<ref name=":3" />

It was previously thought that the southern black korhaan and the [[northern black korhaan]] were the same species (the northern black korhaan being a sub-species, ''E. afra afroaoides'').<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal|last1=Crowe|first1=T. M.|last2=Essop|first2=M. F.|last3=Allan|first3=D. G.|last4=Brooke|first4=R. K.|last5=Komen|first5=J.|date=1994|title='Overlooked' units of comparative and conservation biology: a case study of a small African bustard, the Black Korhaan Eupodotis afra|journal=Ibis|language=en|volume=136|issue=2|pages=166–175|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1994.tb01081.x|issn=1474-919X}}</ref> They are now classified as different species due to their disjunct ranges and habitats, and differences in plumage, vocalizations and social systems.<ref name=":4" /> They also have differences in their [[mitochondrial DNA]].<ref name=":4" />

"Southern black korhaan"  has been designated as the official common name for the species by the [[International Ornithologists' Union]] (IOC).<ref name="ioclist">{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | year=2019 | title= Turacos, bustards, cuckoos, mesites, sandgrouse | work=World Bird List Version 9.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/turacos/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=16 October 2019}}</ref>

== Description ==
The southern black korhaan is a ground-dwelling bird with some [[sexual dimorphism]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|date=1957-01-01|title=Plates I to V|url=https://journals.co.za/content/nfi_annalstm/23/1/AJA00411752_361|journal=Annals of the Transvaal Museum|language=en|volume=23|issue=1|issn=0041-1752}}</ref> The male's main body plumage is of a checkered black and white with a black underbelly and neck.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=kIH_LD3XoBwC&dq=afra&pg=PR24|title=The Birds of South Africa|last=Layard|first=Edgar Leopold|date=1884|publisher=Bernard Quartich|language=en}}</ref> It also displays some white on the underwings, on the cheeks and in stripes over the eyes.<ref name=":1" /> The female, in contrast, has the whole of her head, neck and breast the same colour as her body, which is checkered brown and white.<ref name=":1" />  Both the female and the male have bright yellow legs.<ref name=":1" />

The male is slightly larger than the female.<ref name=":1" /> The male has a [[wingspan]] of 27 cm (10.6 in) to 28 cm (11.0 in) and the female a wingspan of 25 cm (9.8 in) to 26 cm (10.2 in).<ref name=":0" /> The tail is about 12 cm (4.7 in) to 13 cm (5.1 in) long.<ref name=":0" /> The male's bill is larger with a length of 3.8 cm to 3.9 cm (1.5 in), while the female's is about 3.5 cm (1.4 in) long.<ref name=":0" /> The bill is of a reddish colour with a black tip.<ref name=":0" /> Lifespan averages 10 years.<ref name="iucn status" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
The southern black korhaan is endemic to southwestern South Africa, ranging from [[Namaqualand]], south to [[Cape Town]], and east to [[Makhanda, Eastern Cape|Makhanda]]. As of 2020, its estimated suitable habitat throughout its range is approximately {{cvt|20,355|sqkm}}.<ref name="iucn status" /> It prefers open, semi-arid habitats such as shrub-lands and savannahs where it can easily prey on grass-dwelling arthropods, also grasslands adjoining marshland.<ref name="iucn status" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Winterbottom|first=J. M.|date=2010-10-11|title=On the birds of the Sandveld Kalahari of South West Africa|journal=Ostrich|language=en|volume=40|issue=4|pages=182–204|doi=10.1080/00306525.1969.9634346}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Allan|first1=David G.|last2=Harrison|first2=James A.|last3=Navarro|first3=RenéA.|last4=van Wilgen|first4=Brian W.|last5=Thompson|first5=Mark W.|date=1997-02-01|title=The impact of commercial afforestation on bird populations in Mpumalanga Province, South Africa — Insights from bird-atlas data|journal=Biological Conservation|volume=79|issue=2|pages=173–85|doi=10.1016/S0006-3207(96)00098-5|issn=0006-3207}}</ref>

== Behaviour ==
=== Vocalisation ===
Vocalisation has been described as a "continuous cackling sound".<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kemp|first1=A.|last2=Tarboton|first2=W.|date=1976|title=Small South Africa Bustard|journal=Bomakierie|volume=28|pages=40–43}}</ref> It is mostly the male that calls, as it vocalizes very often and very loudly.<ref name=":1" /> The call is a raucous "knock-me-down, knock-me-down".<ref name=":1" />

=== Breeding ===
The southern black korhaan is a [[Polygyny|polygynous]] species, which means males mate with multiple females.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Raihani|first1=Gina|last2=Székely|first2=Tamás|last3=Serrano-Meneses|first3=M. Alejandro|last4=Pitra|first4=Christian|last5=Goriup|first5=Paul|date=2006-04-01|title=The influence of sexual selection and male agility on sexual size dimorphism in bustards (Otididae)|journal=Animal Behaviour|volume=71|issue=4|pages=833–838|doi=10.1016/j.anbehav.2005.06.013|s2cid=53199054|issn=0003-3472}}</ref><ref name=":9">{{Cite book|title=The Birds of Africa, Vol II|last1=Urban|first1=E.K.|last2=Fry|first2=C.H.|last3=Keith|first3=S.|publisher=Academic Press|year=1986|location=London}}</ref> To attract females, males will display high flying.<ref name=":9" /> They breed in the spring.<ref name=":6" /> Females lays one or two eggs in a depression in the soil and covers them with strands of grass. The eggs are of an olive or brown colour with some dark black spots.<ref name=":1" /> Only the females provide [[parental care]] as they take care of the eggs and then raise the chicks.<ref name=":6" />

=== Diet ===
[[File:Southern Black Korhaan (Afrotis afra) male (31992155464).jpg|alt=|right|thumb|Southern black korhaan (''Afrotis afra'') - male]]
This is an omnivorous species. Two-thirds of its diet is made up of [[arthropod]]s, and it will eat termites, beetles, grasshoppers and ants.<ref name=":5" /> The rest of its diet is composed of plant matter, mostly seeds.<ref name=":5" /> It partakes in a mutualistic relationship with ''[[Acacia cyclops]],'' a species of [[Acacia]];<ref name=":8">{{Cite journal|last1=Knight|first1=Richard S.|last2=Macdonald|first2=I. A. W.|date=1991-08-01|title=Acacias and korhaans: an artificially assembled seed dispersal system|journal=South African Journal of Botany|volume=57|issue=4|pages=220–225|doi=10.1016/S0254-6299(16)30942-5|issn=0254-6299|doi-access=free}}</ref> the southern black korhaan benefits from the seeds as they are a readily available food source, and in return, disperses the seeds to good germination sites.<ref name=":8" /> The southern black korhaan will also ingest grit and other small rocks to help in digestion by assisting the grinding process in the [[gizzard]]. A study has also shown that two thirds of birds have nematode parasites in their intestinal tract.<ref name=":5" />

== Status and conservation ==
Recent studies have shown that the southern black korhaan's population has been decreasing lately, which has raised concerns about its conservation status.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Shaw|first1=Jessica M.|last2=Jenkins|first2=Andrew R.|last3=Allan|first3=David G.|last4=Ryan|first4=Peter G.|date=2016|title=Population size and trends of Ludwig's Bustard Neotis ludwigii and other large terrestrial birds in the Karoo, South Africa|journal=Bird Conservation International|language=en|volume=26|issue=1|pages=69–86|doi=10.1017/S0959270914000458|issn=0959-2709}}</ref> The species was once very common but it is now becoming rarer as its habitat is being fragmented.<ref name=":7" /> Hence, it has been deemed vulnerable by the [[IUCN Red List]] of threatened species since October 1, 2016.<ref name="iucn status" />

The primary threat to these species is the conversion of natural vegetation to agricultural lands as well as [[aquaculture]].<ref name="iucn status" /> This has caused [[habitat fragmentation]] and a reduction in available food sources.<ref name="iucn status" /> This reduction in habitat also means less suitable breeding grounds, which has not only affected breeding success, but chick and egg survival rates.<ref name=":7" /> Indeed, agricultural lands do not provide sufficient plant cover to protect them from predators such as the [[pied crow]].<ref name=":7" />

Other threats include climate change, human disturbance and diseases.<ref name="iucn status" />
